data.title: 5 takeaways from House hearing on DHS shutdown's impact
data.description:
Department of Homeland Security (DHS) officials on Wednesday warned lawmakers on the House Committee on Homeland Security about the impacts that the funding lapse that has stretched for 40 days has had on their workers.

The hearing comes as financial strain on Transportation Security Administration (TSA) officers at airports have contributed to waves of callouts and employees quitting after missing their first paycheck almost two weeks ago. President Trump has deployed Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officers to help with lines and add to airport security.

Democratic lawmakers have been against funding DHS in protest against ICE’s tactics in carrying out Trump’s deportation agenda. This escalated after two U.S. citizens were killed by federal immigration officers in Minneapolis in January. This prompted Democrats to call for sweeping reforms to the agency, with some calling for ICE to be abolished.

Trump on Monday backed a new bill that will fund the department but cuts funding for ICE drew ire from Democrats and conservative Senate Republicans. Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.), another critic of the bill, said Democrats would submit a counteroffer.

DHS reported that 90 percent of its workforce is essential and is working without pay.

The shutdown and the hearing all comes after Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin was sworn in, replacing Kristi Noem following her firing earlier this month.

The committee listened to testimony from TSA Acting Administrator Ha Nguyen McNeill, U.S. Coast Guard (USCG) Vice Commandant Adm. Thomas Allan, Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) Acting Director Nicholas Andersen and FEMA Associate Administrator Victoria Barton.

DHS officials emphasize risks to the U.S. as shutdown rolls on

McNeill told the committee that over 480 airport screeners have quit since the start of the shutdown and that the agency is expected to lose $1 billion in missed paychecks by Friday.

She added that the agency is facing a “potential perfect storm” if more TSA officers quit. Adding to the workforce will take time, as she said that it takes between four to six months to certify new officers.

CISA continues to operate with 40 percent of its workforce, remaining focused on operations “generally limited to protecting life and property,” according to Andersen’s testimony. He emphasized that delays in initiating directives while handling cyber threats could benefit U.S. adversaries.

Allan told lawmakers that the USCG has suspended all of its missions except for national security or the protection of life and property missions. He warned that the shutdown has impacted the USCG’s operational readiness and long-term capabilities.

The long stretch of the shutdown has moved the agency “closer to a tipping point,” placing the U.S. at risk of widespread utility shutoffs at bases and exacerbating the USCG’s massive maintenance backlog,” Allan testified. He added that being unable to pay vendors and keep contracts going past April 1 could leave USCG installations vulnerable.

Democrats question ICE’s role at airports amid TSA officer shortage

Reps. LaMonica McIver (D-N.J.) and Troy Carter (D-La.) questioned ICE’s role at airports following Trump’s deployment of federal immigration officers while TSA shortages impact U.S. airports.

McNeill told the lawmakers that ICE officers were trained for “several days” this week to operate security checkpoints. McIver pressed her on this after McNeill said the ICE officers started their training on Monday. The administrator added that officers who have been trained can operate the equipment in order to allow other ICE officers to be trained.

“When we role out operations, it takes a while to really get folks embedded into our operations,” McNeill said. “And it’s gone extremely well, and our feedback from the passengers and our field leadership has been very positive.”

McIver pushed back, recounting that she saw ICE officers “chilling” at Newark Liberty International Airport while also leaving passengers stressed.

Carter raised the timing issue, reiterating that McNeill said it takes four to six months to properly train TSA officers.

“Which spells exactly what I fear,” he continued. “That it’s nothing more than window dressing and cheap theater and political performance to bring ICE agents in, many of which are hardly trained to do the functions of ICE, let alone to come to airports and do sophisticated screening and training.”

McNeill pushed back, saying ICE officers are manning the lines through security checkpoints and are not doing specialized screening.

TSA union leaders have pushed back on this, calling the ICE deployment a “waste of money” and an “insult.” They said that ICE officers have not helped with easing lines through airports

Shutdown hinders DHS’s preparations, training for America 250, World Cup, 2028 Olympics, officials say

Rep. Vince Fong (R-Calif.) asked the officials if the shutdown had any impact on DHS missing any important planning deadlines for international events such as America 250 celebrations, the FIFA World Cup and the 2028 Los Angeles Olympics.

Allan said the USCG has missed milestones but it should only take two and a half days to recover. McNeill told Fong that the shutdown affected TSA’s planning for the World Cup, citing how if the agency sees its workforce shrink, they will have to pivot how training and hiring is conducted.

She added that the shutdown has affected DHS’s counterdrone technologies to be used for the World Cup and America 250.

“With the delay in funding and the shutdown, that is pushing that to the right and we’re running right up to the wire in our ability to procure, deploy and train with these [Counter-Unmanned Aircraft Systems],” McNeill said.

Andersen added that CISA’s upcoming operations were also affected, calling it a “burden… whether it’s for World Cup, for America 250 events or LA 2028 prep, we’re going to continue to see compounding impacts here that are going to affect both the items that require active planning like that and our ability to have a ready workforce that’s going to be able to respond to reactive elements within our threat environment.”

Earlier in the hearing, Andersen said that limitations caused by the shutdown leave CISA limited to performing “essential functions necessary to protect human life and safeguard property.”

Former Homeland Security Secretary Krisit Noem warned last month that the shutdown was affecting DHS’s preparations for those events. She warned that due to the shutdown, the funds from the FIFA World Cup Grant Program had not been awarded.

Correa, Green accuse Trump of holding off funding DHS to prioritize SAVE America Act

Reps. Lou Correa (D-Calif.) and Al Green (D-Texas) both accused Trump of holding off from intervening in the congressional talks to fund DHS to focus on what he has said is his top legislative priority, to pass the Safeguard American Voter Eligibility (SAVE) America Act.

Correa asked the DHS officials if they wanted to see the department’s funded again as soon as possible. All of them said “yes.”

“Apparently President Trump disagrees with you,” the lawmakers said, as a poster board with The New York Times headline reading “‘Don’t Make Any Deal’: Trump Tells Republicans to Hold Firm on Shutdown Talks” rose behind him.

“He actually has told Republican senators, ‘Stop negotiating with Democrats,'” Correa said. “We have a disconnect here, wouldn’t you say? So do we really want to end this shutdown or not?”

Green followed by asking the officials to raise their hands if they think the SAVE America Act caused the shutdown. None of the officials raised their hands. The lawmaker said he asked them this question because Trump “is putting politics above people.”

He referred to The Hill’s reporting that quotes the president when he said on Truth Social that he will not sign any other legislation until the SAVE America Act reaches his desk. Green added that Trump “wants to kill the filibuster” to get the bill passed.

“The president doesn’t care about TSA workers. This isn’t about helping them,” he continued. It’s about getting his way, and he has complete control of the Republican Party. He really does. And given that he controls it, he is demanding that it obey his command to kill the filibuster.”

Green said he wants to defend the TSA workers, calling the deployment of ICE officers at airports “an insult” to TSA officers.

“To somehow conclude that ICE is necessary, not withstanding the fact that a good many members of the flying public are antithetical to this, is an insult to them,” the Texas Democrat added.

FEMA official claiming he ‘teleported’ does not appear to testify

Rep. Timothy Kennedy (D-N.Y.) brought up FEMA’s director of the Office of Response and Recovery, Gregg Phillips, who was expected to attend Wednesday’s hearing. Barton testified in his place. Kennedy appreciated Barton but said he was “really disappointed” Phillips did not appear.

“I wanted to ask Mr. Phillips, along with my colleagues, about his election conspiracy theories. About his violent statements about former President Biden, or his deeply troubling, bigoted comments about immigrants. All of which, to me, make him wholly disqualified to hold his position on its own.

“But only to be outdone by his claims of being teleported to a Waffle House,” Kennedy continued. “It sounds to me that Mr. Phillips is wildly unfit for his role as head of FEMA Response and Recovery, and I look forward to seeing him here before this committee in person.”

Phillips has said on several podcasts that he has been teleported against his will, CNN reported on Friday. He claimed in January 2025 that he was once teleported 50 miles away to a Waffle House in Rome, Ga.

“Teleporting is no fun,” he said. “You know it’s happening, but you can’t do anything about it, and so you just go, you just go with the ride. And wow, just what an incredible adventure it all was.”

Ranking member Bennie Thompson (D-Miss.), who also took issue with Phillips’ past remarks about hitting Biden and saying the former president “deserves to die,” said Phillips did not attend the hearing due to an emergency.

data.title: BlackRock CEO predicting 'global recession' if oil hits $150
data.description:
The CEO of BlackRock warned of a “global recession” if the price of oil hits $150 a barrel amid continuing U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran. 

In a Wednesday episode of BBC’s “Big Boss Interview” podcast, Larry Fink said it’s “too early” to know the long-term effects of this conflict. 

“To me, everybody has to recognize that there’s not going to be an outcome that’s somewhere in the middle,” Fink said. “It’s going to either be two extremes.”

Fink said that following the end of these military operations, Iran could be accepted back into the global trading community and Iranian oil would re-enter the marketplace and stabilize global energy rates. Alternatively, the CEO said that Tehran “remains a threat” in the region, resulting in “years of above $100, closer to $150 oil.”

The executive said the latter option would have “profound implications in the economy.”

“The $40 oil implication is one of abundance and growth, the other one is an outcome of probably stark and steep recession,” Fink said. 

Global energy rates have spiked after Iran launched counterstrikes on U.S. bases and energy production sites in the Middle East, effectively closing the Strait of Hormuz, a major oil trading corridor. 

International benchmark Brent crude was trading at around $103 per barrel on Wednesday, while U.S. benchmark West Texas Intermediate was trading at about $91 per barrel.

The U.S. average price of regular gasoline was up to $3.98 on Wednesday — over a dollar increase from a month ago, according to AAA. 

The Trump administration has attempted to counteract these rising energy prices by implementing several changes, including an increased emphasis on domestic oil production, tapping the U.S.’s Strategic Petroleum Reserves and temporarily lifting sanctions on the sale of Iranian, Russian and Venezuelan barrels already at sea. 

Fink’s comments follow a similar warning from United Airlines CEO Scott Kirby, who told CNBC on Tuesday that he expects high energy prices resulting from the Iran-U.S. conflict to stretch into next year.

“I think if you sort of start looking through what’s happening in the Middle East and how long it’s going to take to recover, even once the Straits of Hormuz are open, it seems like a reasonable assumption for us to make, and so we want to plan for something like that,” Kirby told CNBC’s Phil LeBeau. 

Kirby said the airline plans to decrease flights to prepare for the continuing economic ramifications of this conflict. 

“We hope it’s better, but the cost of planning for something like that is pretty small,” the airline CEO said. “We’re going to do a little less flying than we otherwise would, willing to leave a little bit of demand on the table if oil prices are lower, but want to be prepared for a scenario where oil prices are higher for longer.”

data.title: Bachelorette contestant from canceled season drove car in wreck that paralyzed Madison Cawthorn
data.description:
One of the 22 men vying for reality star Taylor Frankie Paul’s heart on the now-cancelled season of ABC’s “The Bachelorette” was the driver in a 2014 crash that left former Rep. Madison Cawthorn (R-N.C.) paralyzed.

Brad Ledford, 29, was set to appear in the franchise’s 22nd season, which ABC shelved last week following domestic violence allegations against the “Secret Lives of Mormon Wives” star.

Ledford had been described ahead of the season as a “handsome California cowboy” and “fearless romantic” who enjoys “swing dancing, hanging at the beach, and listening to Adele,” according to an archived copy of his ABC biography.

Ledford and Cawthorn, both teenagers at the time, were heading home to North Carolina from a spring break trip in Clearwater, Fla., in April 2014 when Ledford fell asleep at the wheel of his SUV, the Times-News reported at the time.

Their vehicle ran off the road, crashed into a concrete barrier and erupted into flames, leaving Cawthorn – whose legs were reportedly resting on the dashboard – partially paralyzed and in a wheelchair.

Cawthorn claimed in a 2017 speech at Patrick Henry College that his friend left him to die “in a fiery tomb,” a narrative Ledford disputed in a 2021 interview with the Washington Post.

“That statement he made was false,” Ledford told the newspaper. “It hurt very badly that he would say something as false as that. That is not at all what happened. I pulled him out of the car the second that I was able to get out of the car.”

Cawthorn later admitted in a deposition that he had “no memory from the accident.” The pair were estranged for several years but later reconciled, according to the Post.

Cawthorn voluntarily dropped a lawsuit against Ledford in July 2022, months before losing the Republican primary for his House seat to challenger state Sen. Chuck Edwards. Cawthorn had reportedly sought $30 million in damages stemming from the 2014 crash, according to the Asheville Citizen-Times.

It is unclear whether Ledford discussed the crash with Paul or any of his fellow contestants during filming for the “Bachelorette,” but fans likely won’t find out.

ABC scrapped the season just days ahead of its planned March 22 premiere after video surfaced of a 2023 domestic violence incident involving Paul and Dakota Mortensen, the father of her youngest child.

“In light of the newly released video just surfaced today, we have made the decision to not move forward with the new season of ‘The Bachelorette’ at this time, and our focus is on supporting the family,” a statement from Disney Entertainment Television said.

Ledford addressed the cancellation in a post on TikTok this week, saying ABC made the “right decision for right now.”

“I could care less about a TV show not airing right now. There’s real kids involved, real family involved and I want the best for them,” he said in the minute-long video.

“[God’s] forgiven me for many things and he’s already forgiven Taylor, and so I want Taylor to find that peace, and I want her kids to have that peace in the future more than anything, and I think they can,” he added.

data.title: Brennan: Israel 'basically adding fuel to the fires of hostilities'
data.description:
Former CIA Director John Brennan said Tuesday that Israel, through its recent actions in Lebanon and its fighting alongside the U.S. against Iran, has inflamed tensions in the Middle East as the conflict against Tehran rages on.

“At this point, I really think that we need to focus on trying to bring the military operations to a close, not just in terms of the United States and Iran, but also Israel. What Israel is doing now vis-a-vis Iran and Lebanon is just basically adding fuel to the fires of hostilities,” Brennan said on MS NOW’s “Deadline: White House.”

In a post on the social platform X Tuesday, the Israel Defense Forces (IDF) said that they were undertaking “targeted ground operations in southern Lebanon.”

“As long as Iranian intelligence elements continue to operate and advance terror attacks from within Lebanese territory, the IDF will continue to thwart Iranian terrorist activity in Lebanon,” the Israeli military said in a later X post.

On Monday, UNICEF Deputy Executive Director Ted Chaiban said that 324 children had been killed in Iran and Lebanon since the U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran began.

Chaiban said 206 children in Iran and 118 children in Lebanon had been killed since the start of the conflict late last month. He also said that four children in Israel and one child in Kuwait had been killed due to Iranian retaliation.

The region has seen mass death and destruction in recent years, with tens of thousands of people dying in the recent Israel-Hamas war.

On Wednesday, Iran put forward a counterproposal to stop the conflict in the region after shooting down a ceasefire plan supported by the Trump administration. Iranian leaders asked for sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz and a total conclusion to “aggression” from the U.S. and Israel, alongside other demands.

“Iran will end the war at a time of its own choosing and only if the conditions it has set are fulfilled. It will not allow Trump to determine the timing of the war’s end,” a statement by Iran’s Consulate General in Mumbai on X reads.

data.title: More in US say Iran war will make world less safe: Survey
data.description:
More Americans believe U.S. military operations against Iran will diminish, rather than promote, global safety, according to a new survey released Wednesday. 

A Quinnipiac University poll found 42 percent of voters believe the conflict is making the world less safe, while 35 percent say it will increase global safety. Twenty percent of respondents said the military operations will have no effect on safety across the world. 

The survey displayed a stark partisan divide on this question, with 79 percent of Republican voters saying the conflict increases safety, compared to only 2 percent of Democrats. 

The Trump administration launched joint strikes with Israel on Iran at the end of February after failed nuclear deal negotiations, sparking widespread opposition from elected Democrats. 

President Trump has refused to rule out boots on the ground in Iran, and the Pentagon is preparing to send around 2,000 members of the Army’s 82nd Airborne Division to the Middle East. 

Tehran sent over a list of five conditions on Wednesday for ending the conflict after rejecting the U.S.’s ceasefire plan. As part of the proposed deal, the Iranian regime is demanding authority over the Strait of Hormuz, a major oil trading corridor.

Iranian counterstrikes in the Persian Gulf region have effectively halted the flow of oil through the Strait, spiking global energy rates. In the U.S., the national average price of regular gas was up by over a dollar from a month ago on Wednesday, according to AAA.  

The Trump administration has taken several steps to alleviate these rising energy prices, boosting domestic oil production, temporarily lifting sanctions on the sale of Iranian, Russian and Venezuelan barrels and freeing up some of the U.S.’s oil reserves. 

However, less than half of respondents in the Quinnipiac poll said rising gas prices were a “very serious” or “somewhat serious” problem for their household. Thirty percent said the price of gas was “not too serious” and 23 percent said it was “not a problem at all.”

Additionally, only 38 percent of the poll’s respondents said they approve of Trump’s overall handling of his job as president, including 89 percent of Republicans and no Democrats. 

The Quinnipiac poll was conducted between March 19-23, among 1,191 self-identified U.S. voters with a margin of error of approximately 3.6 percentage points.

data.title: Florida Republican introduces bill renaming Palm Beach airport for Trump
data.description:
Rep. Brian Mast (R-Fla.) introduced legislation on Tuesday to rename Palm Beach International Airport (PBI) as “Donald J. Trump International Airport,” just after a month after Republicans in the Florida Legislature voted to do so.

Mast, who represents the Sunshine State’s 21st Congressional District, introduced H.R.8054 in the House Transportation and Infrastructure Committee — chaired by Rep. Sam Graves (R-Mo.).

Florida GOP Reps. Anna Paulina Luna and Daniel Webster, the latter of whom is on the Transportation and Infrastructure panel, have co-sponsored the legislation. The full text of the bill is not yet available on congress.gov.

Last month, Republicans in the Florida House and Senate passed legislation renaming PBI after Trump via votes of 81-30 and 25-11, respectively.

Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis (R) is yet to sign the legislation, while the name change is also subject to approval by the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA).

The name change, if signed into law by DeSantis and greenlit by the FAA, would take effect on July 1, according to the bill.

PBI is roughly four miles from the president’s Mar-a-Lago club. Trump, a lifelong New Yorker, changed his residency to Florida during his first term.

Since the president returned to office, he and his administration have moved to add his name to a slew of buildings and programs, including the Kennedy Center, the U.S. Institute of Peace, the TrumpRx prescription drug platform and “Trump Accounts,” investment accounts that parents can open for their children starting this tax season. 

The Trump Organization, which is run by the president’s sons, Donald Trump Jr. and Eric Trump, last month also filed to trademark the use of the elder Trump’s name on airports.

data.title: Gallego unveils legislation giving unpaid DHS workers bonus
data.description:
Sen. Ruben Gallego (D-Ariz.) unveiled legislation on Tuesday that would give Department of Homeland Security (DHS) employees who have been forced to work without pay during the shutdown a 10 percent bonus when the agency reopens.

The bill would require that DHS employees, including more than 61,000 Transportation Security Administration (TSA) workers, receive a one-time payment from their respective agencies no later than one day after funding is restored.

Many Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officers would not be eligible, according to Gallego, because their pay is still being funded through the billions of dollars secured for immigration agencies in the One Big Beautiful Bill last summer.  

“For weeks on end, TSA agents have been showing up and keeping our families safe. And they’re doing it without a paycheck as Republicans play politics with their livelihoods,” Gallego said in a statement. “They’ve blocked every effort to fund TSA and other DHS agencies, while ICE continues to get paid. These workers deserve to be recognized for their sacrifice.”

Roughly 95 percent of TSA employees are deemed essential and must continue working during a shutdown, according to the DHS contingency plan published in September.

Those employees have already missed one paycheck and are set to miss another come Friday if Democrats and Republicans are unable to reach a compromise to end the funding stalemate.

The financial strain has pushed a growing number of workers to call out sick or quit the force altogether, acting TSA Administrator Ha McNeill told lawmakers on Wednesday during a House Homeland Security Committee hearing.

McNeill said roughly 460 officers have left the agency since the shutdown began on Feb. 14, and callout rates are also surging as high as 40 to 50 percent at multiple airports across the country.

“Some are sleeping in their cars, selling their blood and plasma and taking on second jobs to make ends meet, all while being expected to perform at the highest level when in uniform to protect the traveling public,” she noted.

The staffing shortage, coupled with an already-busy spring break travel season, has created a backlog at airport security checkpoints, with lines stretching several hours at some of the nation’s busiest hubs.

President Trump deployed ICE officers to 14 airports earlier this week to assist with travel disruptions, a move TSA union workers have dismissed as an “insult” and “waste of money” and argue will do little to ease wait times.

TSA has become a flashpoint in the DHS funding fight, which started over Democratic demands for immigration enforcement reform following the fatal shootings of Renee Good and Alex Pretti by federal officers in Minnesota in January.

Senate Republicans have routinely blocked Democratic efforts to pass a standalone bill that would fund TSA and other DHS agencies not responsible for immigration enforcement while negotiations continue.

Billionaire Elon Musk offered early Saturday morning to cover the salaries of TSA workers during the shutdown; however, it was not immediately clear how that would work or if it was legal.

data.title: White House signals 'core objectives' met on war as Iran vows 'relentless' attacks
data.description:
White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt signaled on Wednesday the U.S. was winding down its military operation in Iran, but vowed to ramp up attacks against the country if it does not agree to U.S. terms.

“We are very close to meeting the core objectives of Operation Epic Fury, and this military mission continues unabated,” Leavitt told reporters at the White House press briefing, referring to the decimation of Iran’s navy and the U.S.’s efforts to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

Leavitt referred to the war as a “resounding military triumph,” and noted the U.S. was “ahead of schedule” in the operation.

“That’s why you’re beginning to see the regime look for an exit ramp,” she said. “They recognize they are being crushed, their ability to attack American and allied forces, as well as their ability to defend their own territory is dwindling literally hour by hour.”

But Iranian leaders sounded a much different tune, remaining defiant in the face of U.S. threats.

“Based on some data, Iran’s enemies… are preparing to occupy one of the Iranian islands.  All enemy movements are under the full surveillance of our armed forces. If they step out of line, all the vital infrastructure of that regional country will, without restriction, become the target of relentless attacks,” the leader of Iran’s parliament Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf posted on X as Leavitt was delivering the day’s briefing.

Leavitt, while trying to sound a somewhat diplomatic tone, also said the regime has an opportunity reach a deal that included abandoning nuclear threats, or else. 

“The remaining elements of the Iranian regime have another opportunity to cooperate with President Trump, permanently abandon their nuclear ambitions, and cease actively threatening America and our allies,” Leavitt said. “But the president’s preference is always peace. There does not need to be any more death and destruction.”

“But if Iran fails to accept the reality of the current moment, if they fail to understand that they have been defeated militarily and will continue to be, President Trump will ensure they are hit harder than they have ever been hit before,” she said. “President Trump does not bluff, and he is prepared to unleash hell.”

She said it was Iran’s “miscalculations” that have caused the destruction of their military and leadership and that their actions will decide if there is more “violence” moving forward.

Leavitt said the “productive conversations” being had with Iran is what led to the postponing of strikes on Iran’s power plants and energy infrastructure.

Trump earlier this week said in a TruthSocial post that attacks on Iran’s power plans and other energy infrastructure would pause for the week while negotiations took place. The Iranians, however, maintain that no such negotiations are taking place.

Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, meanwhile, said Tuesday that “we negotiate with bombs.”

“You have a choice as we loiter over the top of Tehran as the president talked about, about your future,” Hegseth said standing next to Trump in the Oval Office during a swearing-in ceremony. “The president has made it clear that you will not have a nuclear weapon, the War Department agrees, our job is to ensure that, and so we’re keeping our hand on that throttle as long and as hard as is necessary to ensure that the interests of the United States of America are achieved on that battlefield.”

But Iran’s response has suggested that the talks aren’t productive – or even taking place. The Middle Eastern country Wednesday flat-out rejected the U.S.’s 15-point ceasefire proposal, opting instead to produce their own.

Trump’s proposal, delivered by Pakistan, addressed Iran’s ballistic missile and nuclear program, the Strait of Hormuz, which has been restricted since the start of the war on Feb. 28, leading to fluctuating oil costs.

Tehran instead asked for a complete end to aggression, war reparations, region-wide ceasefire, and sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz. A document shared on social platform X by the Iranian Embassy in India, showed that Iran considered the U.S. proposal to be  “a deception aimed at escalating tensions.”

Neither the U.S.’s nor Iran’s proposal have a chance of being adopted by the other without major changes, instead they highlight the opposite sides the two countries are on when it comes to striking a deal.

data.title: 8 key figures who will — and won't — be at CPAC
data.description:
Conservatives are descending on Grapevine, Texas, this week for the annual Conservative Political Action Conference (CPAC), but a smattering of notable names are expected to skip the event that’s long served as a pulse check for the GOP. 

President Trump is reportedly set to miss the gathering for the first time in a decade, while Vice President Vance is also missing from the speakers’ list after giving remarks last year. And while Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton (R) will speak, his rival in the Lone Star State’s high-stakes primary runoff, Sen. John Cornyn (R-Texas) will be staying in Washington, D.C., despite calls from CPAC leadership to show up. 

The timing of this year’s gathering in Texas comes amid rifts over the Iran war and frustrations within the party ahead of the midterms as Republicans fight to keep control of Congress this fall. Last-minute decisions could change the lineup, but the absences stand out at the event that’s been a key political gauge for Trump and the GOP.

Here are some notable names on and off the speakers’ list for this week’s conference, as of Wednesday afternoon: 

Who won’t be speaking:

President Trump

Trump is set to skip out on CPAC for the first time in a decade, Time reported this week, citing a White House official who cautioned that Trump’s schedule could change at the last minute. 

The president will speak at a Saudi-led investment conference on Friday before spending the weekend in Mar-a-Lago, according to the Palm Beach Post. 

Though Trump has held onto his conservative base’s support, his approval rating has taken a hit amid voter frustration over the economy and new concerns about the war in Iran — and CPAC’s speakers will take the stage against that backdrop. 

The Democratic National Committee (DNC) was quick to point out Trump’s absence as CPAC kicked off on Wednesday. 

“Now, as his approval ratings plummet to historic lows — even by his own standards — he’s hiding from what should be a room filled with his strongest supporters,” DNC rapid response director Kendall Witmer said in a statement. 

Vice President Vance

Vance has addressed CPAC several times since he burst onto the political scene but, like Trump, he had not been announced as a speaker when the 2026 conference started on Wednesday. 

He debuted at the conference during his 2022 Senate run, came in low on a CPAC straw poll to become Trump’s running mate in 2024, then hammered home his America First bonafides in a speech as vice president in 2025.  

As chatter builds about 2028, CPAC’s annual straw poll could offer a temperature check on how Republicans feel about Vance as Trump’s heir-apparent. 

Sen. John Cornyn (R-Texas)

The Texas Senate Republican primary runoff between Paxton and Cornyn is in spotlight, but only Paxton is set to take the CPAC stage this week. 

CPAC put pressure on Cornyn to appear, sharing a call to “get it scheduled” on the social platform X. 

But Cornyn’s campaign told The Hill that the incumbent senator is not attending and expects to be in DC “for votes to pass DHS funding and the Save America Act, of which he is an original sponsor.”

After multiple years in the DC area, CPAC is taking place this year in the Lone Star State as the bitter primary fight stretches into a May runoff — and as both candidates court a coveted Trump endorsement. 

Elon Musk

One of the most notable figures at last year’s CPAC was tech mogul Elon Musk, who appeared on the stage wielding a chainsaw as he led the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) to make drastic workforce reductions and funding cuts throughout the federal government. 

Mere months later, Musk’s alliance with Trump imploded as the world’s richest man exited DOGE and dissented over Trump’s “big, beautiful bill” legislation. Now, he’s among the names absent from the 2026 CPAC lineup. 

Who will be speaking:

Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton

As Paxton fights with Cornyn for the GOP Senate nod in Texas, he’s slated to be speak at the Ronald Reagan Dinner, a prime slot at CPAC. 

Senate Republicans’ campaign arm has backed Cornyn and raised alarm that a Paxton primary win could open the door for a rare Democratic flip. Trump after the primary said he’d endorse one candidate and ask the other to drop out, but the president has stayed out of the race and Paxton has dug his heels in. 

A pro-Paxton super PAC launched a TV ad in the West Palm Beach media market as Trump visited Florida earlier this month, seen as courting a Trump endorsement. The deadline to formally drop out of the race passed last week, meaning both candidates will appear on the May ticket regardless of a status change.

Paxton is among a handful of other GOP Senate hopefuls who are slated to take part in this year’s CPAC, including Republican National Committee Chair Michael Whatley from North Carolina, Rep. Mike Collins (Ga.), Rep. Kevin Hern (Okla.) and businessman Nate Morris of Kentucky.

Steve Bannon

Former White House strategist and podcaster Steve Bannon is among a number of conservative activists and media figures who are set to take the stage this week. 

Bannon, a longtime Trump ally and host of the “War Room” podcast, has warned that the president’s war in Iran could be a political drag on the president and the party. 

Conservative podcaster Benny Johnson, right-wing commentator Jack Posobiec, Newsmax host Rob Finnerty and YouTuber Nick Shirley are also set to speak to the attendees. 

White House border czar Tom Homan

White House border czar Tom Homan will address the crowd amid a shakeup in the administration’s leadership on immigration and a shutdown of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS). 

Trump fired Kristi Noem from her role as DHS secretary earlier this month, and the Senate confirmed her replacement, former Sen. Markwayne Mullin (R-Okla.), this week. 

Meanwhile, Homan said on Monday that federal immigration officers will make arrests at airports, where they are assisting Transportation Security Administration (TSA) officers amid the shutdown. 

Other top administration officials, including Education Secretary Linda McMahon, Small Business Administrator Kelly Loeffler and Federal Communications Commission chair Brendan Carr, are on the schedule, as is Jay Bhattacharya, director of the National Institutes of Health and Acting Director of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC). 

Former Rep. Matt Gaetz (R-Fla.)

Also in the CPAC lineup is Matt Gaetz, a former Florida congressman who was once Trump’s pick to lead the Department of Justice before he withdrew from consideration amid controversy and allegations of sexual misconduct. 

Now the host of a program on the conservative “One America News Network,” Gaetz, who briefly eyed a run for governor in this year’s midterms, will appear in Texas just days after Democrats flipped two Sunshine State Legislature seats, adding to their winning streak in districts Trump carried two years ago.

data.title: TSA chief warns staffing problems could close airports, threaten World Cup
data.description:
The top official at the Transportation Security Administration (TSA) warned Congress on Wednesday that staffing shortages tied to the partial government shutdown could force the agency to close small airports and undermine the country’s readiness for this summer’s FIFA World Cup.

In testimony before the House Homeland Security Committee, Ha Nguyen McNeill, the senior TSA official performing the duties of the administrator, said 460 TSA officers have quit since the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) ran out of funding on Feb. 14. More than 1,100 Transportation Security Officers (TSOs) left during the 2025 shutdown. 

Coupled with rising call-out rates, attrition among TSOs has driven what McNeil described as “the highest wait times in TSA history,” which she said could compel the agency to make tough decisions, including closing some airports.

“We are being forced to consolidate lanes and may have to close smaller airports if we do not have enough officers,” she said in her opening remarks. “It is a fluid, challenging and unpredictable situation.”

Later in the hearing, McNeil was asked if smaller airports are at risk of closing temporarily or operating at reduced capacity.

“As the shutdown drags on and we see increases in call-out rates, we’ll have to make real-time decisions on how we operate,” McNeil responded. “For example, we have many small airports that operate one or two lanes, and a few call-outs could really severely impact our ability to run the operations. 

“In those scenarios, we would not be able to open the checkpoint at the airport,” she continued. 

The TSA chief also warned that high attrition among TSOs will have “dire” consequences for the country’s ability to prepare for the FIFA World Cup, scheduled from June 11 to July 19 across 16 North American cities, including 11 in the United States. 

McNeil said TSA is expecting the World Cup to bring in between 6 million and 10 million additional air travelers this summer. She expressed concern that the agency will lack trained officers to handle the influx, noting there isn’t enough time to start training new recruits. 

“As the shutdown drags on, we fear we will continue to lose talented and experienced employees to other jobs that can provide a steady paycheck,” McNeil said. “Not only is the shutdown decreasing the number of interested candidates, for those we are able to hire, they are required to complete four-to-six months of training before they are certified to work at checkpoints.”

“At this point, newly hired officers will not be able to work on the checkpoint until well after the 2026 FIFA World Cup,” she continued. 

“This is a dire situation,” the TSA chief added. “We are facing a potential perfect storm of severe staffing shortages and an influx of millions of passengers at our airports for the World Cup games in less than 80 days.”
